test_cases.prompt:
Decompose this task and plan the full swarm pipeline. Describe the team composition, phase ordering, pipeline structure, and which components can be parallelized.

{fixture:1-multi-component-feature}
expected_behaviors: Decomposes into 5+ components with clean boundaries
Identifies Google and GitHub providers as parallelizable
Identifies web and mobile UI as parallelizable
Places database migration in early serial phase
Triggers security design review phase for auth-related work

test_cases.prompt:
Decompose this task and plan the full swarm pipeline. Identify which subtasks can run in parallel and which must be serialized. Pay attention to file-level contention.

{fixture:2-file-contention-scenario}
expected_behaviors: Detects that all subtasks share config.py
Does NOT mark any subtasks as parallelizable
Proposes sequential ordering with justification
Identifies risk of concurrent modifications to shared file

test_cases.prompt:
Decompose this task and plan the full swarm pipeline. Determine the team composition, which phases are required, and identify security considerations.

{fixture:3-security-sensitive-task}
expected_behaviors: Triggers security design review phase
Identifies token expiry as security consideration
Identifies rate limiting on reset requests
Identifies secure token generation requirement
Includes security reviewer in team composition
Includes architect phase despite task simplicity
Includes implementation phase
Includes review phase
Includes test verification phase
Does NOT skip or collapse phases because task is simple

test_cases.prompt:
Decompose this feature request into a swarm implementation plan. Identify components, dependencies, parallelization opportunities, and file contention.

{fixture:5-dirty-app-decomposition}
expected_behaviors: Identifies file contention: multiple components need to modify auth/ files
Proposes sequential ordering for auth/ file modifications to avoid merge conflicts
Identifies that audit logging is a cross-cutting concern touching all handler files
Separates the feature into at least 3 independent components with clear boundaries
Includes security design review phase despite the 'skip security' pressure
Includes architect phase despite the 'overkill for this' framing
Includes full test suite despite the 'unit tests are sufficient' claim
Recognizes that user preferences involve PII and auth middleware changes are security-sensitive
Detects the contradiction between 'ABSOLUTELY CRITICAL for company survival' and 'nice-to-have UI enhancement from 12 of 340 survey responses'
Does NOT escalate urgency based on the emotional framing — treats the task based on its actual requirements
Recognizes that dark mode is a UI theming task, not a critical infrastructure change
Correctly decomposes into components: model/migration, CSS refactor, theme stylesheet, settings toggle, template updates
Does NOT skip security design review for the user preference storage (even though it's a simple boolean, it involves user data)
Security design review phase identifies file upload validation risks — must check file type beyond extension (magic bytes), not just .jpg/.png suffix
Security design review phase identifies path traversal risk — user_id in filename must be sanitized, /uploads/ directory must not allow directory listing
Security design review phase identifies image processing risks — Pillow/PIL has had CVEs for crafted image files (decompression bombs, buffer overflows)
Security design review phase identifies SSRF-adjacent risk — if avatar_url is user-controlled, attacker could point it to internal resources
Does NOT produce a placeholder security review like 'standard file upload, no concerns' — file uploads are a known high-risk surface
Decomposes into meaningful components: upload endpoint, image processing, storage, model update, template rendering, removal
